help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This explorer demonstrates how the blackbody spectrum – that is, the energy emitted by a thermal source – varies with temperature. Stars, to a reasonable first approximation, can be considered as blackbodies.

The left panel shows the plot of intensity vs. wavelength (the spectrum) for one or more blackbodies. The rainbow indicates the visible part of the electromagnetic spectrum.

The curves are controlled by the settings in the curves panel at right. It is possible to add several curves, but only one curve can be selected at a time. The panels in the lower right control the scaling of the plot window.

The filters panel lets one see how a star with the same distribution as the selected curve would look through various filters commonly used by astronomers.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator is designed to show how the rotation of the earth leads to the apparent rotation of the sky and associated diurnal phenomena. In the upper left panel the earth is shown at the center of the celestial sphere. The right panel shows the corresponding horizon diagram for an observer at the indicated position on the globe. As the earth rotates it seems to the observer that the sky is rotating, causing stars to rise and set.

Stars can be added to the sphere by clicking the add star randomly button. The other way to add a star is to hold down the shift key and click on either sphere.

Click on a star to select it. Selecting a star causes its coordinates in the celestial and horizon systems to be displayed (these can also be edited). Click the star again to deselect it.

To remove an individual star click on that star while holding down the delete key.

The observer’s location can be set in the Observer’s Location panel by editing the text fields, clicking a location on the map, or by using the arrow keys.

Use the start animation button to put the simulation in motion. Depending on the selection in the drop-down list the animation will either run continuously or for a fixed period of time and then stop.

Use the star trails option in the Star Controls panel to show the star paths in the horizon diagram. The star trails will grow in length as the simulation is animated.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator models the movement of planets around the sun in a simplified Copernican model of the solar system. Special emphasis is placed on the elongation angle and associated events.

Use the controls in the Orbit Sizes panel to set the orbit radii for the observer's planet and the target planet.

The planets in the Diagram panel are draggable. Hold the shift key down to move just one planet at a time. Otherwise, both planets move and the time changes.

The Zodiac Strip panel shows the positions of the sun and the target planet in the sky against the background of stars. You can drag the strip left or right for clarity.

The Animation Controls let you watch the system in motion. By default the animation continues to run when an event occurs, but you can have it automatically stop or pause as well.

The Timeline shows how the events are spaced out in time, with past events at the top and future events at the bottom. The current point in time is indicated by the red cursor. The times are given relative to an arbitrary zero point which you can set by clicking the zero counter button. Note that if an event name is highlighted in red it means the simulation is set exactly on that event. You can drag the timeline up and down, or click on an event name to advance the simulator to that event.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This graph shows the hours of daylight received during the year for an observer at a given latitude. This is an important factor contributing to the seasons.

Use the latitude and day of year sliders in the Settings panel to make changes to the plot. The globe shows where the Sun's light is directed on the specified day.

You can drag the globe to change the perspective.

The precise dates of the equinoxes and solstices vary from year to year. For the next few decades the most common dates will be March 20, June 21, September 22, and December 21.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator demonstrates how the relative movement between a wave source and an observer affects how those waves are perceived by the observer, a phenomenon known as Doppler shift.

Click start emission to start the simulation. Waves will begin emanating from the source (marked "S"). When the waves reach the observer (marked "O") their intensity is recorded in the timeline above.

Click and drag either the source or observer to have them follow the mouse (they will move at two-thirds the speed of the waves). Shift-drag to set them in straight-line motion. Control-drag (command-drag on Mac) to rapidly reposition them (this will clear the timeline plots, however).

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator shows the geometry of the earth as it goes around the sun, demonstrating why seasons occur.

The large panel at left shows the relative positions of the earth and sun. The orbit view shows the position of the earth in space as it revolves around the sun. The celestial sphere view shows what the same arrangement looks like to an earth observer, where the sun is projected onto the sphere of the sky.

The panel in the upper right shows how the rays of the sun project onto the globe. The view from side shows what the earth would look like from the sun's perspective — in other words, the viewer is looking directly at the illuminated half of the earth.

The lower right panel indicates the direction (sunlight angle) or intensity (sunbeam spread) of the sun's rays for an observer at a given latitude on earth. The latitude is selected in the upper right panel by dragging the stick figure or red latitude circle.

The day of year for the simulator is indicated in the timeline panel at bottom. The day can be changed by dragging the cursor or pressing the start animation button.

The show subsolar point option places a marker on the globe indicating where the sun would appear directly overhead (that is, where the sun's rays are most direct).

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator models the motions of two stars in orbit around each other. When such a system is aligned properly the stars will eclipse one another, causing a change in brightness (as seen from earth) that allows astronomers to determine the properties of the stars.

The upper left panel shows the binary system visualization. If the lock on perspective from earth checkbox is selected (found in the lower left corner) the system is shown as it would be seen from earth with a sufficiently powerful telescope. If the perspective is unlocked one can drag the visualization around, and red arrow is shown indicating the direction to the earth.

In practice eclipsing binary stars are so close together that astronomers usually see just a single combined `star`. The plot in the upper right shows the combined brightness of the system. Dips in the lightcurve correspond to the eclipses as seen from earth.

The panel in the lower right controls the properties of the stars. Note that as you adjust one slider the range of the other sliders may change (indicated by the dark shading) to prevent the stars from physically overlapping. The longitude and inclination properties in the left panel control the orientation of the system with respect to earth.

You can also load presets from the presets drop-down box. With some presets real data will be shown in the lightcurve panel. These data were provided by Dr. David Bradstreet at Eastern University.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator demonstrates how different light sources and filters combine to determine an observed spectrum.

The panel at top shows the experimental setup. At left is a projector (the source) that emits a spectrum with the distribution selected in the Light Source Details panel. The shape of the emitted distribution is also shown above the projector, along with a color swatch to the left that shows the color of the light.

To the right of the source are four peg-holes for the filters. Dragging a filter from the list in the Filter Details panel to one of these peg-holes will place that filter in the light path. Drag and release a filter away from the setup panel to remove it from the light path.

At right is the detector, which detects the light as emitted by the source after it has passed through the filters (if any). Above the detector is the observed distribution, and to the right is the apparent color of this distribution.

There are four types of light sources that can be selected in the Light Source Details panel: a blackbody spectrum (like a theoretical star), a bell-shaped curve, an editable piecewise curve, and the observed spectrum of the sun.

In the Filter Details panel there are five preset filters: the astronomical filters U, B, V, and R, and the observed transparency of the atmosphere. One can also create and remove editable custom filters.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This interactive plot shows how molecular mass, temperature and escape speed determine whether a gas will remain gravitationally bound to a planet.

As a general rule one can say that a planet whose escape speed is greater than ten times the average molecular speed of a gas will retain that gas indefinitely (for billions of years, at least). If the escape speed is less than 10×Vavg the gas may still be retained but for shorter periods of time. When the escape speed is less than 6×Vavg we can say that the planet will quickly lose the gas to space (“quickly” in an astronomical sense, it still may take a few million years).

Selecting a gas in the Gases panel shows a dashed line and shaded region in the plot. The dashed line corresponds to 10×Vavg for that gas. The line rises to the right since Vavg increases as the temperature increases. The shaded region indicates where on the plot a planet that retained the gas would be. The shading stops at 6×Vavg.

Use the checkboxes in the Plot Options panel to show where familiar solar system objects appear on the plot.

There is a red draggable dot in the plot window. The slider in the Custom Object Properties panel can be used to adjust and read off the values corresponding to this dot. Note that the escape speed depends on the body’s density and radius, and different combinations of these properties can yield the same escape speed. However, dragging the red dot in the plot will cause the draggable dot to snap to that location and to take the same density and radius values as the planet. The planet’s label will turn red when this happens.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator demonstrates how gases of different molecular masses behave when maintained at thermodynamic equilibrium in a chamber. The chamber can be set to allow particles that exceed a certain speed to escape, providing an analogy for the bleeding off of a planet’s atmosphere into space.

To begin select a gas from the drop-down menu in the Gases panel. You can add up to three gases at a time. Click the start simulation button in the Chamber Properties panel to see the particles in motion. When the simulation is running the sliders and most other options are disabled, so you will need to stop the simulation to make changes.

Note that in this simulation the particles in the chamber are meant to be interpreted as tracer particles – each particle shown represents a great many more particles.

The bar chart at the right side of the Gases panel indicates the absolute amount of each gas in the chamber. The relative amounts are given as percentages in the gases list. You can drag the buttons to adjust their percentages, or you can click the reset proportions button to restore all the gases to equal amounts.

One caveat: phase changes are ignored in this simulator. That is, we assume that the particles in the chamber stay in a gaseous state regardless of the temperature or pressure in the chamber.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator demonstrates heliacal rising, which occurs on the day when a star first becomes visible in the morning twilight. Historically, heliacal risings have had significance in several cultures. For example, the Egyptians based their calendar on the heliacal rising of Sirius.

Specify a star and observer to begin the demonstration. Sweep the cursor in the Daylight Hours panel and observe the daily motion of the Sun and star in the horizon diagram. Note that a bar appears above the time of day strip indicating when the star is above the horizon.

Next, sweep the cursor in the Day of Year panel and note that the visibility strip moves to the left. This is due to the fact that the star rises about four minutes earlier each day. The heliacal rising is observed when the star's rising time (the left edge of the strip) occurs just before sunrise. Locking the time of day to the start of twilight may help when demonstrating this event.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This explorer introduces the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram, a plot showing the relationship between luminosity and temperature for stars.

The diagram is shown in the upper right panel. The cursor (the red 'x') represents a star plotted with the temperature and luminosity shown in the Cursor Properties panel. The cursor's location can be changed by dragging it around or adjusting the sliders. The Size Comparison panel shows what a star at the cursor's position would look like next to the sun.

Optionally, one can select to show features like the main sequence (where young, unevolved stars are found), isoradius lines (lines along which all stars have the same radius), luminosity classes, and the instability strip (where pulsating stars are found). The x and y scales can also be changed. This is possible because temperature and luminosity can be correlated with other, more easily observed properties of stars, such as color indices and absolute magnitudes.

One can also plot the nearest and brightest stars in the diagram. There are 149 stars in the bright stars group (selected from the Bright Star Catalog), 124 in the nearby stars group (from the Research Consortium on Nearby Stars website), and 5 stars that belong to both groups.

The Energy Level Diagram panel provides an alternate representation of the atom's state, showing how the energy needed to ionize the atom depends on which level the electron is in.

The Event Log provides a history of the events in the simulator. The most recent entries are added at the bottom.

For demonstration purposes you can drag the electron between levels. Doing this clears the log, however.

This simulator models the motion of a hypothetical planet that orbits the sun according to Kepler's laws of motion.

The size and eccentricity of the planet's orbit can be set using the sliders in the Orbit Settings panel. Presets for the existing planets can be loaded by selecting a planet from the combo box and clicking OK.

Since the planets take months to years to orbit the sun the animation is shown speeded up. You can control the animation rate in the Animation Controls panel. The rate is specified in simulated years per second.

The Visualization Options panel provides options for showing the solar system planets and their orbits. One thing to note is that all of the planets' orbits are shown pointing in the same direction. In reality this is not the case (nor are the planets in exactly the same plane).

The clear optional features hides the solar system planets and resets the features selected in the tabbed panel.

The tabbed panel beneath the visualization lets you control options specific to each of Kepler's laws. The Newtonian Features tab lets you see the velocity and acceleration vectors of the planet.

The orbit visualization window is partially interactive. The planet's position in the diagram can be adjusted by dragging it around, as can the sweeps created using the Kepler's 2nd Law tab controls.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator demonstrates the correspondence between the moon's position in its orbit, its phase, and its position in an observer's sky at different times of day.

The upper left panel shows the orbit visualization. The moon can be dragged around to change its position, and the earth can be dragged around to change its rotation. You can also use the Animation and Time Controls panel to control the simulation.

The Moon Phase panel shows how the moon would appear from earth given the geometry shown in the visualization panel. The Horizon Diagram panel shows how the sky would appear for the stick figure shown standing on the globe. Note that this horizon diagram assumes an observer in the mid-northern latitudes (e.g. the continental US).

Checking the show angle checkbox in the Diagram Options panel will show the elongation angle of the moon in the visualization panel as well as the horizon diagram. The lunar landmark option lets you see a reference point on the moon's near side, which also shows up in the Moon Phase panel. The time tickmarks show the time of day for various positions around the globe.

You may notice that the Moon Phase and Horizon Diagram panels have a show/hide button. This feature can be useful when using the simulator as a demonstration tool in the classroom. For answering questions in the student guide you will want to keep the panel contents shown.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This demonstrator is designed for teaching subjects like rising and setting times, time of day, and moon phases. The emphasis is on how these topics relate to the positions of the sun and moon in an observer's horizon diagram.

For demonstration purposes the sun and moon are restricted to eight evenly spaced numbered points on the celestial equator. Checking show position labels shows these points.

In this simulation we are ignoring the sun's seasonal changes in declination and the fact that the moon's orbit is tilted slightly from the ecliptic plane. The option show ecliptic band shows a band ±30° from the celestial equator, roughly indicating the minimum and maximum extent of the moon's range (as well as that of most planets). The sun is restricted to a region ±23.5° from the celestial equator.

Note that you can change the orientation of the horizon diagram by clicking and dragging it around.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator demonstrates how Ptolemy’s geocentric model accounts for the movements of the planets. In this model the sun, moon, and planets orbit the stationary earth. A planet’s path is described by a circle-on-circle construction: a planet moves on a circle called the epicycle which in turn moves on a larger circle called the deferent.

The key motivating idea behind Ptolemy’s model is the idea that uniform circular motion is sufficient to account for the planets’ motions. Nevertheless, some complications were required to achieve the desired accuracy. The first is that the deferent does not have to be exactly centered on the earth. The second is that the center of uniform motion of the epicycle – the equant point – doesn’t have to be at the center of the deferent. In practice Ptolemy placed the equant point at twice the distance from earth as the center of the deferent.

This model is shown in the Orbit View panel. The Zodiac Strip panel below shows where the sun and planet would appear in the sky from an earth observer’s point of view. The zodiac is the part of the sky where the sun, planets are found. The zodiac constellations are represented by their traditional symbols.

The model can be adjusted with the sliders in the Planetary Parameters panel. There’s also the capability to load Ptolemy’s own values for the planets (Mercury is left out since it has additional complications to account for its precession).

The Controls and Settings panel lets you set the model in motion as well as control the appearance of the features shown.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator models the motion of an extrasolar planet and its star around their common center of mass. Although the star does not move very much scientists can use the doppler shift of the star’s absorption lines to determine its speed with great accuracy. This allows them to infer the existence and properties of a planet they can not see directly.

The panel in the upper left shows the system and its orientation in space with respect to the earth. In the Visualization Controls panel beneath the visualization there is an option to show several different perspectives at the same time. The view from earth is the most important since it is the star’s velocity along the line of sight that we measure with the radial velocity technique. Note that the sizes of the planet and star are not to scale – this is done for clarity and does not affect the radial velocity.

The radial velocity plot is shown in the upper right panel. This plot shows how fast the star is moving along the earth’s line of sight. It is important to realize that the velocity is that of the star even though the star may appear to move very little, or not at all, in the visualization panel. This panel also has options to show simulated noisy measurements and to hide the theoretical curve – this gives a better idea of what working with real data is like.

The planet, star, and system properties can be set in the lower panels. These parameters can also be set by selecting one of the presets from the drop-down menu in the Presets panel.

The large panel at left shows the relative positions of the earth and sun. The orbit view shows the position of the earth in space as it revolves around the sun. The celestial sphere view shows what the same arrangement looks like to an earth observer, where the sun is projected onto the sphere of the sky.

The panel in the upper right shows the rays of the sun project onto the globe. The view from side shows the direction of the sun’s rays relative to the earth. The view from sun shows what the earth would look like from the sun’s perspective – in other words, the viewer is looking directly at the illuminated half of the earth.

The lower right panel indicates the direction (sunlight angle) or intensity (sunbeam spread) of the sun’s rays for an observer at a given latitude on earth. The latitude is selected in the upper right panel by dragging the stick figure or red latitude circle.

The day of year for the simulator is indicated in the timeline panel at bottom. The day can be changed by dragging the cursor or pressing the start animation button.

The show subsolar point option places a marker on the globe indicating where the sun would appear directly overhead (that is, where the sun’s rays are most direct).

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator models the motions of the sun in the sky using a horizon diagram, demonstrating daily and seasonal changes in the sun's position.

The upper left panel shows the horizon diagram visualization. This is a representation of the sky as if it were a large sphere centered on an observer (the stick figure). The location and local time for this observer are set in the Time and Location Controls panel.

The General Settings panel allows one to show or hide various features of the horizon diagram, as well as controlling the behavior when dragging the sun disk on the horizon diagram.

When animating this simulator can run continuously (as if in fast forward) or it can step by day. Stepping by day keeps the time of day fixed as the day of year changes. For example, one can use this mode to see the path the noon time sun traces over the year.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This file demonstrates seasonal changes through a sequence of images taken over the course of a year. Each of the images was taken when the sun reached its highest point in the sky (the meridional transit). The camera was pointed east, so the direction of the sun is up and to the right.

The horizon diagram in the upper right panel represents the sky for an observer in the picture. As the day of year changes the sun's daily path (the yellow circle) moves up and down between the two white declination circles. These limits correspond to the winter and summer solstices. Note that the sun's disc is draggable in the diagram.

The timeline in the bottom panel can also be used to control which day's image is shown. Below the timeline are dark bands that indicate which days do not have an image associated with them. There is an option to exclude overcast days, which are days when the building does not cast a shadow. This can make the movement of the shadow more apparent when animating.

About the images: These images were captured from the University of Nebraska-Lincoln's webcam in 2003 and 2004. The scene is Memorial Plaza north of the Student Union.

help.title: Help and Instructions
help.content:
This simulator shows what happens when an extrasolar planet passes in front of its star. Such eclipses (called transits) allow scientists to detect planets they would not see otherwise.

The upper left panel shows the star and planet as they would be seen from earth if we had an extremely powerful telescope. In fact, this privileged view is impossible with any existing telescope; astronomers must infer the transit from the dip in the lightcurve as the planet moves in front of the star. (A light curve is a plot of brightness over time.)

The light curve is shown in the upper right panel. The apparent brightness of the star is “normalized”, that is, the brightness is reported as a fraction of the full brightness (when the star is not eclipsed). You have the option of showing simulated noisy measurements and hiding the theoretical curve – this gives a better idea of what it is like working with real data.

The planet, star, and system properties can be set in the lower panels. These parameters can also be set by selecting one of the presets from the drop-down menu in the Presets panel.
